meta.title: Seres del Ecuador — Diablo Huma & Cucurucho
meta.description: An immersive cultural platform about two icons of Ecuadorian heritage: the Diablo Huma and the Cucurucho.

portal.title: Beings of Identity
portal.subtitle: Two figures that define the Ecuadorian soul. One born of sun and earth. The other, of faith and silence.

ch1.title: The Two-Faced Being
ch1.body: The Diablo Huma — whose name in Kichwa means 'Devil's Head' — is not a demon in the Western sense. It is a sacred figure of Ecuador's indigenous Kichwa people, guardian of the cycles of the sun and the wind.
ch1.quote: Not the devil of the conquistadors. It is the indomitable spirit that no time could silence.

ch2.title: The Inti Raymi
ch2.body: The Festival of the Sun is celebrated each June, around the solstice. The Diablo Huma leads the dance carrying a chicote — a ceremonial whip — with which it cleanses the path of bad energies. The dancer can perform for up to four consecutive days, as an offering to Taita Inti (Father Sun).
ch2.quote: The earth shakes when the Diablo Huma dances.

ch3.title: Twelve Horns, Two Faces
ch3.body: The twelve horns represent the twelve months of the year. The two faces embody Andean duality: Hanan Pacha (the upper world) and Kay Pacha (the present world). The colors of the costume — red, green, yellow, blue — are the Andean chakana woven in wool.
ch3.quote: Neither face looks back. Both move forward, simultaneously, in all directions.

ch4.title: A Living Tradition
ch4.body: The Inti Raymi is celebrated today in the provinces of Imbabura, Pichincha, and Cotopaxi. Communities such as Otavalo, Cayambe, and Latacunga keep the dance alive. The tradition faces the challenge of preserving itself without becoming a tourist spectacle.
ch4.quote: Every June, the páramos remember who we are.

ch1.title: The Anonymous Penitent
ch1.body: The Cucurucho is the most iconic figure of Quito's Holy Week. Dressed in white, with a conical hood covering the entire face, it marches in silence carrying an enormous wooden cross. Under the hood there is no identity: the rich and the poor are equal before God.
ch1.quote: The silence is so deep you can hear the white robes brushing against the centuries-old cobblestones.

ch2.title: The Good Friday Procession
ch2.body: Every Good Friday, thousands of cucuruchos descend into Quito's Historic Center for the procession of Jesús del Gran Poder — the oldest and most massive in Latin America. The route passes through 16th-century colonial churches: La Compañía, San Francisco, La Merced.
ch2.quote: They walk barefoot over the colonial cobblestones.

ch3.title: The Personal Vow
ch3.body: Many cucuruchos fulfill a promise made in moments of anguish or gratitude. Anonymity is essential: the hood erases social rank. Some carry crosses weighing up to one hundred kilograms. Others drag chains. It is the payment of a sacred debt.
ch3.quote: Faith has no surname beneath the white hood.

ch4.title: Quito, Living Heritage
ch4.body: Since UNESCO declared Quito's Historic Center a World Heritage Site in 1978, the procession has also become an act of urban identity. The 21st-century cucurucho coexists between authentic devotion and the gaze of international tourism.
ch4.quote: Tradition is not preserved in a museum. It is walked.

final.title: Earth and Faith
final.subtitle: A Single Identity
final.body: The Diablo Huma and the Cucurucho are not opposites. They are Ecuador's two pulses: the indigenous heartbeat from the Andes and the mestizo heartbeat from the colonial churches. Both dance, both fall silent, both carry something sacred.
final.coda: What does it mean to be Ecuadorian? The answer lies in both.

quiz.title: Which one are you?
quiz.subtitle: A cultural exploration exercise
quiz.disclaimer: This quiz is a playful cultural resource with no ethnic or diagnostic implications.

dh.title: You are Diablo Huma
dh.body: You carry the earth and wind within. Your spirit is free, dual, untamed. Music and dance are your language.

cu.title: You are Cucurucho
cu.body: Your strength comes from silence and faith. You carry what others cannot see. Your devotion is your deepest identity.

questions.q: How do you express what you feel?
questions.a: By dancing, moving, with my body

questions.q: Do you prefer to celebrate in?
questions.a: A plaza, in sunlight, with music
questions.b: A church or space for reflection

questions.a: June — the solstice and the festival
